items.correct: Gradually increasing training demands so the body keeps adapting.
items.misconception: Training as hard as possible immediately.
items.why: Students think 'overload' means 'maximum'. It means slightly exceeding current capacity - too much causes injury; too little stalls progress. The key word is PROGRESSIVE.

items.q: What does the 'I' in FITT stand for?
items.why: Students guess. FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type - the variables you adjust to apply progressive overload. Intensity = how hard (e.g., % of 1RM or heart rate).

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: Performance levelling off in a programme is called a:
items.why: Correct answer: Plateau. “Threshold” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which document screens an athlete's readiness to train?
items.why: It's PARQ. “BMI chart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Minimum rest days per week typically recommended for a balanced programme?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
